title: Offer and acceptance
summary: 9084 Contract — invitation to treat, communication, revocation, and acceptance rules.
learningObjectives: Distinguish offers from invitations to treat.
Explain communication and revocation of offers.
Analyse acceptance rules: mirror image, postal, instantaneous.
Apply cases: Carlill, Partridge, Hyde v Wrench, Brinkibon.

simpleExplanation.title: Agreement through offer and acceptance
simpleExplanation.summary: An offer is a firm promise to be bound; acceptance must match exactly and be communicated — special rules apply to post and email.
steps: Offer — specific, communicated, intent to contract.
Postal rule — acceptance on posting (Adams v Lindsell).

flashcards.back: Offer — firm intent to be bound on acceptance; ITT — invites others to make offers (shop displays: Fisher v Bell).
When communicated to offeree before acceptance — even if offeree has not read it (Dickinson v Dodds).
Acceptance must match offer exactly — counter-offer destroys original offer (Hyde v Wrench).
Acceptance effective when posted, not received — Adams v Lindsell; exceptions: instantaneous communication (Entores).
Bid is offer; auctioneer accepts by fall of hammer (Sale of Goods Act 1979 s57).
By performance of required acts — no separate communication of acceptance needed in unilateral contracts.

sections.content: **Offer and acceptance** (3.1.2) — core for problem Qs. Work **offer → acceptance → timing**.

sections.question: A sells land to B for £100,000. B replies offering £95,000. A refuses. B then writes accepting the original £100,000 offer. Has a contract been formed?
sections.solution:
**Original offer:** A's offer to sell at £100,000 is a valid **offer**.

**Counter-offer:** B's reply offering £95,000 is a **counter-offer**, not acceptance — it **destroys** A's original offer (*Hyde v Wrench*).

**Attempted acceptance:** B's later letter accepting £100,000 is **not valid acceptance** — there is no live offer to accept. B's counter-offer was itself an offer that A rejected.

**Conclusion:** **No contract** formed. B would need A to make a **fresh offer** at £100,000.

sections.label: Mark an offer and acceptance question
